message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To confirm the incidence of perioperative SARS-CoV-2 infection and associated mortality after surgery.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design and setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Analysis of routine electronic health record data from National Health Service (NHS) hospitals in England.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We extracted data from Hospital Episode Statistics in England describing adult patients undergoing surgery between 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    January 2020 and 31
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    October 2020. The exposure was SARS-CoV-2 infection defined by ICD-10 codes. The primary outcome measure was 90-day in-hospital mortality. Data were analysed using multivariable logistic regression adjusted for age, sex, Charlson co-morbidity index, index of multiple deprivation, presence of cancer, surgical procedure type and admission acuity. Results are presented as n (%) and odds ratios (OR) with 95% confidence intervals.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We identified 1,972,153 patients undergoing surgery of whom 11,940 (0.6%) had SARS-CoV-2. In total, 19,100 (1.0%) patients died in hospital. SARS-CoV-2 infection was associated with a much greater risk of death (SARS-CoV-2: 2,618/11,940 [21.9%] vs No SARS-CoV-2: 16,482/1,960,213 [0.8%]; OR: 5.8 [5.5 – 6.1]; p<0.001). Amongst patients undergoing elective surgery 1,030/1,374,985 (0.1%) had SARS-CoV-2 of whom 83/1,030 (8.1%) died, compared with 1,092/1,373,955 (0.1%) patients without SARS-CoV-2 (OR: 29.0 [22.5 −37.3]; p<0.001). Amongst patients undergoing emergency surgery 9,742/437,891 (2.2%) patients had SARS-CoV-2, of whom 2,466/9,742 (25.3%) died compared with 14,817/428,149 (3.5%) patients without SARS-CoV-2 (OR: 5.7 [5.4 – 6.0]; p<0.001).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The low incidence of SARS-CoV-2 infection in NHS surgical pathways suggests current infection prevention and control policies are highly effective. However, the high mortality amongst patients with SARS-CoV-2 suggests these precautions cannot be safely relaxed.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Summary boxes</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What is already known on this topic</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>High mortality rates have been reported amongst surgical patients who develop COVID-19 but we don’t know how this compares to the concurrent surgical population unaffected by COVID-19.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>Strict infection prevention and control procedures have substantially reduced the capacity of surgical treatment pathways in many hospitals.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>The very large backlog in delayed and cancelled surgical procedures is a growing public health concern.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What this study adds</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>Fewer than 1 in 100 surgical patients are affected by COVID-19 in the English National Health Service.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>Elective surgical patients who do develop COVID-19 are 30 times more likely to die while in hospital.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>Infection prevention and control procedures in NHS surgical pathways are highly effective but cannot be safely relaxed.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Mortality after surgery with SARS-CoV-2 infection in England: A population-wide epidemiological study
